turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Number 405, Leroy Powell, appellant versus Texas.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Hutt.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: I would like to defer to Mr. Tom Davis, a member of this bar and of the State of Texas bar to move the admission as pro hac vice.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Permission is granted.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. I move the admission pro hac vice of Mr. Don Davis of Texas to argue for appellant in this case.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Motion is granted.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Thank you Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Davis.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. The question presented by this appeal is whether the conviction of a chronic alcoholic while being found in a state of intoxication at a public place is prohibited by the Eighth and Fourteenth Amendment of the United States constitution. The case comes to the Court in this posture. On December 19, 1966 the appellant was charged in the corporation of court of the city of Austin, Texas with the offense of being intoxicated in a public place. He was tried and found guilty and he appealed the conviction to the county court at law of Travis County before following a trial de novo, he was again convicted and fined $50. At the conclusion of the trial, the county judge -- the county court under these circumstances and in this particular case being the court of last resort in Texas, entered findings of fact with conclusions of law and the appellant perfected a direct appeal to this Court. I would like to point out the outset that appellant does not here contend that Article 477 is unconstitutional on its face. What we do contend is that as applied to appellant, Article 477 is unconstitutional in that application. Appellant being a chronic alcoholic, afflicted with the disease of chronic alcoholism who can not resist the constant excessive consumption of alcohol and whose appearance in the public is involuntary. Appellant's story is typical of the stories of many Americans afflicted with this disease, and I might add many indigent Americans. At the time of the trial, he was 66 years old, and he had a history of drinking dating back till 1925. He was injured on the job in the middle of 50's, injured his back. He's been unable to do heavy physical labor since. He drinks everyday namely wine and beer. He gets drunk to the point where he passes out only once a week. In short, his drinking is limited only by his financial resources. His financial resources are derived wholly from the money he can pick up shining shoes -- excuse me -- in a local bar. This averages approximately $12 a week. In fact, in 1966, the fines imposed on him for punishing him for this disease exceeded 20% of his total income. The arrest records in Travis County alone reflect 73 arrests since 1949. This is only part of the story.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: All for public drunkenness?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: All except for one. Back in 1951 or 1952, there was one for disorderly conduct, but all of the arrest are merely for public drunkenness, excuse me. He estimates that in Travis County and in Bastrop County together, he's been arrested approximately a hundred times. But again let me emphasize that this is only a token expression of evidence of the degree to which this man drinks.The number of times he's intoxicated as he says “I've been very lucky in avoiding police arrest.”

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did he have any sober moments to talk?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir, he does. There's no question about that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What sort of (Inaudible)?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: The record shows that he becomes intoxicated to the point before he passes out only once a week. The morning before this trial began, he had had a drink. He had no more money to buy any more liquor. But the record shows he drinks each and every day. I would not represent to the Court that he becomes totally intoxicated everyday. Plus I don't think I can justify it unless there's some very benevolent alcoholic on his team. He doesn't have the money to get drunk everyday, but I think I can represent to the Court that his man's alcoholism and his alcoholic content was kept at a very high level each and every day, it doesn't take --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: The statute does not provide for imprisonment at all does it?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Your Honor, under Texas procedure if you do not have the finance to pay your fine, you're imprisoned and you work it out.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: And here that has happened to him?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir that is his normal procedure.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: What is the equivalence between the dollar amount and the day of imprisonment in Texas?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: I believe it's $5 a day, I thought I was four to five. It's $5 a day.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: The average fine that the record reflects is been imposed on Mr. Powell is been $20.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: So that'd mean four days in jail if he couldn't pay it?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Four days in jail, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does the record show (Inaudible) has been institutionalized?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: He has never been institutionalized. The record does reflect it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Are there any institutions in the county where he's arrested that he could be treated in?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes Your Honor, the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Do they have a program there for treating him?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: No, Travis County is the county where the state capital sits without -- Travis County is the county where the state capital sits Austin, Texas. We have the state hospital. Since 1953, there has been comprehensive civil commitment legislation on the books. This has never been funded, to my knowledge no one has ever been civilly committed for public drunkenness in our county.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is that throughout the state?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: I would assume so. The study that I have appointed with from Houston does not reflect a practice of civil commitment in the State of Texas.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: So as far as you know there are no medical facilities in the state where a man like this could be treated?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Excuse me, I misunderstood you question. No, there are medical facilities available. That was my reference to the state hospital in Austin.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well of course I suppose a private institution where a man could, but that's rather quite expensive, but I mean for an indigent like this who has the illness that you claim. Is there any place where the authorities are accustomed to treating people that kind or could treat them?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir, in Austin there is a facility which is in the experimentation stage.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: How long has it been there?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: About -- I was talking to the director of it about a month ago, and they had just got it started. And they had just developed -- gotten the facility for a halfway house. Now this isn't a pure medical facility.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Are commitments there voluntary or may there be some involuntary

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: This is wholly voluntary.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Wholly voluntary.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: This is wholly voluntary.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Yes, so in other words you county authorities then cannot -- no course of procedure by which they can commit them to treatment.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: It's on the statute. There is a statute where a county judge on his own motion can do it.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: But it has not been is that it?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: To my knowledge it has not, and let me be quick here to add that I would hesitate and be very reluctant to recommend wholesale civil commitment of these indigent alcoholics. And I say that for a number of reasons. First of all, the people that have been charged with the duty of studying alcoholism and its facets, the people who are expert in the area and as reflected in the D.C. Crimes Commission and the U.S. Crime Commission report. These people recommend voluntary treatment. They say it will work. Also, before the Court and cited in my reply brief is a study in the late issue of the FBI bullet, reflecting a pilot project in the City of St.Louis where on a voluntary basis, 97% of the chronic alcoholic stayed for the entire period prescribed by the medical profession. So what -- there is evidence that voluntary treatment will work, so I hesitate to suggest to the Court that a wholesale program of civil commitment is desirable.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Mr. Davis, I don't want to anticipate of course your argument, but I would like to know at some point just what you think should be done, or what you should would happen if the police came on the petitioner here on the public street and he was dead drunk. Just what do you think should happen in that event?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Should be taken to a medical facility. The man is laying on a street suffering from an epileptic seizure, the police don't pick him up and take to the jail. He's suffering from a disease. Now maybe under some mistake, the police might, but if a drunk is laying on the street --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Well let's suppose he's not, let's suppose he's walking around. He's navigating but he's obviously intoxicated, and then you think the police can come up to him and say “Come along with us.” And what is that? Is that an arrest or is it a --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: No sir I don't believe it is.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: -- stop or, it's not a stop in prison but a stop to sniff or what?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Police have two duties. Of course they have a duty to enforce the crimes, but they also have a charge with the duty to protect the health and welfare of the citizen of the community. Now certainly there's no false arrest by pick up a -- assuming I'm a police officer. If I pick up a heart attack victim and took him to a medical facility for treatment, neither can every false arrest if I picked up a chronic alcoholic who is in a drunken stupor wondering down the street.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I know but suppose he says “I don't want to go.”

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Well I don't think the person in that condition can make that judgment.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I mean you can't make them, well suppose he does anyway even though you think he can't?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Again, I believe the power of the police extends to protect the health of the public citizens.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: That's kind of a difficult question though isn't it?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: It isn't once we accept chronic alcoholism is a disease.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Alright, I know of no --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: I read a --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: -- is there any rule of law that apart from custom general opinion of mankind that permits a policeman to pick up somebody who is sick and take him up.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Well excuse me. I think was overstating my case to you -- a more narrower question that I had anticipated. No, unless a chronic alcoholic is in such a condition that he's a danger to somebody, I see no purpose in picking him up, unless it's just to take him as you would for any one suffering to some place where he could get help. But here again let me emphasize, it's the medical facility where he should be taken not the police station.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: How hard --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Aren't there so many communities so that don't have any medical facilities in this day regardless of what the humanities of this -- of such cases are? Is there any humane way that we can treat these people to for instance to keep them from staggering out into the street and getting hit by automobiles or crossing in front of a red light and getting killed? There're so many places in the country that have no medical facilities at all and the only place they could take them would be to jail and call for a doctor I suppose to give them some kind of immediate treatment. What are we going to do in all these communities where the states have never awakened to the need for doing something in this area other than to just lock them up for 48 hours or 4 days or whatever it is, and then letting them out to do the same thing?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: There can be no question that there's going to be some temporary dislocations where this Court to hold down. You can not criminally punish for a disease or for involuntary exhibiting its symptoms in public.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Of course the --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: There will be temporary dislocations.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Doesn't your argument go far to be saying that they may not criminally punish? You must also say that they may not arrest, and unless you say -- well you just say may not arrest, period.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes, yes.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: And if the -- if they're danger to themselves and somebody else, you say they should be taken to a hospital?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: No, no. I do not say that if they're involved in some other anti-social behavior --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: I didn't say -- I just said it's a -- here's a gentleman who maybe hopelessly drunk on a street and he maybe in danger to himself, his condition maybe dangerous to himself.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: I thought awhile ago you suggested he should be taken to the hospital?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir, I do.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Against his will?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Once again, I don't think a person in that condition can rationally refuse medical health. I don't think he can make a rational --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: What will be -- how soon at the hospital may he decide he wants to leave?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Well I think when they sober him up.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: When? How long is that?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Whatever it takes medically to --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: You mean like 48 hours or 4 days?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: No sir, I don't think it takes that long. I really don't.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: But in any event, your argument is that they may not arrest them and take them to jail?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: So it's just an imposition of criminal punishment is they may not take them to the -- take them -- arrest and take to jail or even take them to the jail.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Well, we need to differentiate between two classes here of people here. First of all, the known alcoholic --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: And your just -- I gather your argument is just addressed to chronic alcoholic?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Not just to people who are drunk.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well how -- who determines where there's a chronic alcoholic, the policeman?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: No sir, that was what I was going to point out. There's going to be many, many instances. The police obviously can not make a diagnosis of chronic alcoholism on the street. He will take him to the station.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Take him where?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: To the station.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: -- to arrest?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes, yes.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Against his will.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes. I don't think we can charge the police with the duty of being able to make a diagnosis for chronic alcoholism, but when his disease condition comes to light, and in many, many instances this is going to come to light at booking desk.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: But not in this case there's not much question about this --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: There's no question at all about it.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: -- at this person and they don't need to take him to the station to find out because they're well acquainted with him.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: So you woud say they had no power to arrest this man?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Unless in the sense to take in for punishment.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Well let me see if I get your proposition correctly. Are you arguing that constitutionally states may not establish a crime of public drunkenness, or are you arguing only that if one is a chronic alcoholic that he may not be prosecuted for the offense of public drunkenness?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That's right. That is what I --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well then that's to say then if, as I understand it, if an officer comes upon a person who is drunk, and if there is an ordinance that makes an offense -- makes an offense of public drunkenness then the officer may arrest him, correct?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Now, do I understand you're purposing this, he comes across and know him chronic alcoholic?

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: No, no he comes across a person who is drunk.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Who is drunk, okay.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And he may arrest him --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: -- for the offense of public drunkenness.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: But -- and take him to the police station.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: But once he gets him there, he may not prosecute him for the offense -- well he may prosecute him for the offense of public drunkenness, but the defendant will have a defense of chronic alcoholism, is that it?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well then I don't quite understand the relevance of many of our -- of this discussion of --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: I think we're focusing --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: -- take him to a hospital first as well.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Well I think we're focusing on a very narrow part. What I was trying to emphasize is that our society doesn't let us or doesn't permit us to punish for suffering from a disease. What I was trying to emphasize at here, society is punishable for exhibiting the symptom of that disease.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Yes, but I want to get clearly that you're not disputing the constitutionality of a public drunkenness, ordinance or statute nor on its face nor on of the arrest under that of a person who is drunk on the street and his prosecution for that offense. And you're limiting this solely to the defense which maybe in oppose to chronic alcoholism.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That is correct. It is a very narrow issue before the Court.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: But if he attempts to cross a known alcoholic, a chronic alcoholic, may he do the same thing?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Your Honor, certainly we would hold that he would pick him up and take him to a health facility.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Well I know but you're arguing about the constitution, I suppose and the --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Constitutionally, I don't believe it would be a false arrest, because as I stated a moment ago to the answer Justice Fortas' question, they have a power not only to enforce the rules, but also to protect the health of the citizens. Now certainly when they pick up a person if he is of a danger to himself and takes him to a health facility, surely that could not be constitutionally attacked.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Is there any other instance in the law that you know of where it makes that kind of a distinction between the right to prosecute or right to arrest?It sounds I -- it seems to be something that --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Well I'm not saying --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: -- is a noble and I'm not at all sure that anyway that it would achieve the objective that you desire.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: I am not saying the police have the power to arrest.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Well then, how do they take -- what do you call it when they pick up the fellow and he says “I don't want to go.”

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Let's focus on it this way.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: And they take him up anyway and take him to the precinct.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Let's focus on it this way. Let's look at an insane person. What does the police do there?

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Well that's good.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: If a man is insane and he's out in public, they pick him up and they take him to the health facility. They don't put him in jail.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: No, are you sure of that?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Well the defense of insanity is raised.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: The defense of insanity is raised quite a bit.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Asserted by the police -- before the police officer?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And he tries him for insanity?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: No, what I'm saying is defense of insanity is raised is a defense to many crimes. But we're talking here about an insane person.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I think I agree with that Mr. Davis, but the policeman takes a risk that he may be prosecuted in that instance. Where that police authority comes from other than the doctrine of public and private necessity has always interested me and I'm not sure what its origin is. But if somebody -- if some member of the family has a psychotic episode in the house, let's say.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: And the, is -- sister or brother or mother or father calls the police, the police come out then and they see that this person is obviously having a psychotic episode. Then the policeman if he's being a good public servant will take the person and call an ambulance to take the person, transport him, kicking and screaming if necessary, to a hospital. Now, let's say socially -- I've always thought that that was socially necessary function of the police.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: I do too.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I'm not sure where the authority comes from.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: I do too.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Well isn't that about what you're talking about here, that is to say. And I'm a little surprised that you made the concession that the police would be perfectly justified in taking a person over to the precinct, because if they've justified the taking an alcoholic to the precinct and necessarily follows a sergeant at the desk is no more confident than the policeman is to make judgment as to his medical condition. So the next thing to do is to toss him into drunk tank then when he comes up for trial, if he's lucky enough to have a public spirited lawyer then the defense can be made, medical testimony adduced and would be proved to be a medical case. That's not what you're after him. That's not the problem is it?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: No, it's not. It goes too far. What we're trying to do here basically is to transfer what is a medical problem to the public health facilities. Get it out in a way that the criminal courts --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: The time to put this on the same basis as in the person who has a psychotic episode and he's taken off to the hospital. In that kind of case, I suppose a policeman in theory at any rate takes a risk when he picks up somebody on the street for public drunkenness, he takes a risk. May be the person is having a, or is just recovering from an epileptic fit or as in attack of amnesia or something else, a patient. And the policeman takes a risk. Now what you're suggesting here if it's unconstitutional to prosecute a person who is an alcoholic in the medical sense then I would think that it's unconstitutional to arrest him. Now do you disagree with that? Do you think that's --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes I --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: -- an extreme view?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: -- I do. I don't think the conclusion is compelled by the analysis. I do believe that what we're after and I don't say to the Court that it's the police should take all drunks to the police station, I do not say that. And I didn't mean to concede that they can and should take chronic alcoholics to the police station. What I was saying was that if they know a man is chronic alcoholic take him to the medical facility. If it comes to light placed at the booking desk that he's got a history of 500 arrests which is common in this country, immediately transfer him to a medical facility.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: And you're not talking laws are you? You're talking --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: No, I'm talking of practical application.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: You're talking of good conduct and this is not a court of conduct.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: I had thought that your point was considerably narrower. As I understand the question as verbalized on page 3 of your brief that it is that it's a violation of the United States constitution to convict a chronic alcoholic upon the offense of public intoxication. Not that it's unconstitutional to arrest somebody who is publicly drunk nor is it a constitutional violation to prosecute somebody who's publicly drunk, but simply that it's not constitutional to convict a chronic alcoholic of that offense. And that I would presume would be an affirmative defense to be yet opposed in a prosecution of somebody for public drunkenness. He would have a defense by saying “Well I'm a chronic alcoholic.” At that I understood with your case but I'm quite confused now with your colloquy.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That is correct and I confused the issue by talking practicalities of how we get to that point, is what I did.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: I must say Mr. Davis, I don't have any statistics, but I would imagine that there's more public drunkenness of non-alcoholics than there is of alcoholics in the sense that you're talking about.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: I think that's correct, but here is the point. There is no justification for criminally punish a chronic alcoholic.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well I'm just wondering though in terms of the ultimate of a -- what in effect you're urging is that society ought to come around to the view that the chronic -- the alcoholic who was in that sense has the disease ought be treated medically and -- society ought to provide facilities and it has to be a public -- a burden because largely the instance of this disease is with people who can't themselves afford the expense of the treatment, isn't that right?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That is correct, yes sir it is.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: But the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Do you make any distinction between these situations and narcotic addicts who are obviously under the effect of narcotics publicly?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That of course is a difficult question and it's -- I think we can talk about it in general conclusion but we don't focus on the issues. Now --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Why not --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: -- focusing --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- why not focus on the issue.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: -- let's focus on the issue. That's what I wanted to do.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Let's focus on the issues. If a person were proved to be an addict, he was proved to that he was suffering from the disease if his use of narcotics was involuntary and if he was picked up for using narcotics in a situation where the fact showed he was not peddling it or he was not involved in any other social behavior, then I think the Court could conclude under those circumstances that the law is punishing him for a symptom of his disease. I don't think it's compelled. I think the Court may very well draw a terrible distinction between conduct where the social consequences are so great as in narcotics use, and in conduct such as this where it's chronic alcoholism. These people are typically harmless. They're non-belligerent and passive --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And yes, are they always typically harmless? Don't they do many things that aren't very large number of our thefts in all of communities in the country don't they come from these people who are braking open cars and doing things of that kind on the street in order to get money to buy alcohol?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Your Honor, all I can do is cite the authorities to the Court which I'm familiar with. The president commission on law enforcement and justice concluded and I cite in my brief, there are as yet no data that demonstrate that alcoholism is a significant factor in the commission of crimes.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well all they have to do is go to any police court and see these -- a lot of these people who are often arrested for public drunkenness, also arrested for petty theft and things of that kind.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Well I think it's important here that I distinguish what a person, an alcoholic, even if he is a chronic alcoholic. If he's being in -- if he's involved in anti-social behavior, certainly we have the power to civilly commit him, so society will be protected in any event.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I understood you as far as that's concerned but you said these people do anything except just be drunk in public. Now that isn't factual for my own observation for many years.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: I don't --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I've had experience of seeing police force in cooperation --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Well I think this would be a very good point for me to defer to the amicus counsel who will state in some detail exactly what the Court is positioned on right now. So with that in mind, I will at this time defer to the --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you a question --

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- just to see if I understand. Then I understand what you have said that a man who is a chronic alcoholic can not be constitutionally convicted of a crime. That's basically what you're presenting isn't it?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: No sir, for the crime of public intoxication.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well I'm -- yes for the crime of public intoxication.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But he can not constitutionally be convicted, but if the policeman comes along and sees the man, you -- it seems to me it gotten awfully considerably, duty of arrest. If a policeman comes along and sees a man that it's completely insane, no doubt he's crazy, and he sees him shooting somebody on the street, he would sudden if you justified an arrest if he would.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir, he would.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And the defense would be later set up.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And the policeman would be absolutely justified in making the arrest.

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Because I understand it that's what you've been trying to argue?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes sir and I think it's wholly applicable to alcoholism too. My earlier discussion simply got off at my fault on the practicalities of what we want in the future.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well then as I understand it, there is in your argument, there is no question as to the right of a policeman to arrest a man for being drunk in public, that there's no question about his right to put him in jail for that offense. There is no objection to his being tried for the offense of public drunkenness, but that when it comes to his trial he has a right to interpose the defense of being a chronic alcoholic. And if the court finds or the jury finds that he is a chronic alcoholic then he must be treated medically instead of criminally. Now is that a fair statement of your position?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: Yes it is.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And do you go any farther than that?

turns.name: TOM DAVIS
turns.text: No sir I do not.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Thank you very much.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. I represent the nine amici curiae who are filed the browns colored brief in this case.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: May I ask you at the outset if you disagree at all with what counsel had just stated in answer to my question.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: I would pose one qualification, Your Honor. There have been times where a given individual was arrested as the U.S. Crime Commission found on one occasion, some 31 times in four months largely by the same police officer. I think there may arise on occasion where there is an abuse of the arresting officer's discretion where he obviously has no probable cause for the arrest. I'm not saying that this would necessarily be to --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You mean because he's a chronic alcoholic?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Because he is a known chronic alcoholic, for example, a man in the District of Columbia where that incident took place who had been adjudicated by court to be a chronic alcoholic and had been acquitted on 31 prior occasions. The -- I think at that point the arresting officer if he knows that would -- well he may have not probable cause for the arrest, but certainly if he did not know that the man was an alcoholic, I would agree 100%.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well does a police officer when he's charged with the administration of law of that kind, have a right to judge the man's defense and either convict him of in his own mind and take him to jail, or acquit him on the street even though he is publicly drunk in violation of an ordinance?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: As I say Your Honor, I am not an authority under law of probable cause for arrest and I have not researched that in detail. I merely raised that as one possibility that certainly the U.S. Crime Commission did raise a question about it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did they raise the question of constitutionality?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: They did not discuss it in constitutional terms.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, that's what we're dealing with here.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That's what we're dealing with here.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, but other than that, you agree thoroughly with what --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: I certainly do, yes.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I suppose if it's unconstitutional to convict a person who is an alcoholic in the medical sense, then it is unconstitutional to arrest a person who is an alcoholic in the medical sense. Now when you come to appraising whether the policeman did or did not do something for which he is legally liable then you get into a set of very different considerations. If he knew that the person whom he arrested was a medically alcoholic then he has done something that maybe capable. If he did not know that and had no reason to know it then he is not, he has not done something that is medically capable. But if it's unconstitutional to convict a person who is medically alcoholic, then I suggest for your comment that it is unconstitutional for the police to arrest such a person.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: I'm a little bit --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Then you get all of these questions as to the capability or non-capability of the opposite.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Well, Mr. Justice Fortas I am troubled by your analysis a little bit, and I'd like to use the analogy to the insane. I would suggest that it would be unconstitutional in this day and age to convict an insane person for a product caused by his insanity. I would not suggest it would be unconstitutional to arrest him and charge him with a crime. Therefore, I would be inclined to agree with the Chief Justice in that analysis.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, yes I'm not sure.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Because you have questions.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I'm not sure that I agree with you there, but I think too that those are very different considerations.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: We'll recess.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: --private agencies and trained or professional personnel as well as lay persons. They're deeply interested, all of them, in improving both law enforcement and the standard of healthcare in our society. The Chief Justice raised the question just before we adjourned, about the lack of facilities to handle the public inebriate alcoholic in our population. I can't speak specifically for the state of the argue in Texas, but certainly around the country, the amici represent the people who would have to take up the slack and who must provide that treatment. The American Medical Association, the state programs in alcoholism. These are the people who will be dealing with the problem. They're prepared to undertake the treatment. One of the things that has been found throughout the country is that in areas where the jails have hidden the problem up to now, the situation is very poor. There is a lack of treatment, but as soon as the jails are not used to hide the problem, treatment becomes available largely to a better use of existing resources.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: We think it unnecessary --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: -- the police can't disturb a person (Inaudible) do anything to him except take him to a hospital.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Well I think --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: I think there is a tenable position that has been urged to the Court in the brief on that very point. We think it unnecessary to go that far in this case because we think this presents a very simple case of an illness.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible) individual known to the police officer already have a record (Inaudible) drunk at the street, take him in the drunk tank, and the only thing that your (Inaudible) is that they must be under the government policy allowed the defense of chronic alcoholism so it --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: I think this raises a very important question of what this means to a community with a ruling that is handed down, for example in the District of Columbia, in Philadelphia and Atlanta, Georgia and in the Fourth Circuit. What has happened here is that as a result of the court in the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia circuit holding that alcoholism is a defense to the charge of public intoxication. The police and the health authorities have devised plans that are beginning to circumvent the arrest process completely. We now --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: As to any person?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: As to any person. And the -- what will happen is that these people as recommended by the U.S. and D.C. crime commissions will be all be taken directly to a detoxification center. There is a Bill in Congress now that has passed the House of Representatives that repeals the District of Columbia public intoxication law.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: That might be the practical result of the court's decision --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- but you're not implying that that is constitutionally required are you?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: No, we are not arguing that point in this case Your Honor.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: All you're arguing is that chronic alcoholism is a defense to this charge?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That is the entire issue in this point, in this case, but I did want to answer the question that was posed.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That have -- about.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Tremendous reform, and frankly those are the reforms that the amici --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Not completely, no.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: They -- well let me suggest that in 1960 -- fiscal year 1966, there were 47,000 arrests for public intoxication, and that had remained steady for about five years. In fiscal '67, I have the preliminary figures which show 37,000 that's a decrease -- of 10,000 arrests. I would suggest, and that was before Your Honor --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: I do not. Atlanta was notorious as being worst than the District of Columbia in terms of arrest and I have no information on that area or Philadelphia. But Philadelphia, the diagnostic and relocation center has submitted both a brief to this Court which I believe is a yellow colored brief, and also a report of a 10-day study entitled Alternatives to Arrest, which points out that what the way they obviously would head under their decision would be to circumvent the entire arrest problem. I would like --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: What is the authority of the police to say in the District of Columbia not to arrest but to -- against the person's will take him to someone to facilitate. What is the authority?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: At the present time Your Honor?

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: The authority and at this at one time was debated in the District of Columbia as to whether there was a common law authority to pick up an incapacitated drunk on the street and take him to a health -- to a hospital. In the D.C. Crime Commission report, the commissioner's unanimously concluded that there was common law authority to do this under the -- as a civil law matter and that it would not constitute a criminal arrest. They used the example of taking an insane person or a heart attack victim to a hospital. Nevertheless, they went on to say that if there was any doubt about this and if the police were concerned, certainly the police should be protected by a statute.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: What -- and what's the limit of -- this is of course if think and --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: -- and as a practical matter, what are the limits of the power to hold? Do you wait -- do just -- do sober a person up or do you treat him? If you just --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: I would say --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: -- you just sober him up then turn him loose?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: I would think, the way I would analyze it and this is my personal view point, is that the state is justified in intervening in a parens patriae view or rather role anytime that the individual is incapacitated for any reason, whether it's drunkenness and whether he's an alcoholic or not.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: But that once he becomes in a position where he is, you might say sufficiently in possession of its faculties to make a rational decision which has to be a medical judgment that he should be allowed either to leave or to stay. Now I think it's interesting that in the places where detoxification had been used and in -- particularly in the VERA Institute Project that has just been started in New York. The people who are given the opportunity of treatment as contrasted with the old process of arrest --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: This being voluntary.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: This being completely voluntary, and in fact there's not -- they aren't even allowed to be brought in by the police in the VERA Institute Project. Under those circumstances, I don't think they've had anybody or any significant number maybe 2 or 3% that have refused to come in voluntarily and wanted to stay there as long they would be allowed. And the main reason they've never been treated before. These people are under fringes of society. They've never been allowed to enjoy any of the health benefits that the rest of the population have.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That is correct. It has passed the House.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: It is, yes, there is a citation to it and to the hearings held on the House side. The Senate hearings have not yet been held.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: What would you say is the -- be the situation (Inaudible) --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Undertake the responsibility for this treatment.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: That's -- assuming that you don't claim to have a personnel in the facilities now?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: No, certainly not, and I think the brief for the amici points out in some veil of detail that we don't have the facilities today. We don't have all the treatment personnel today. We don't have all the plans. The reason is that communities have never been awaken to the problem and that we have found that with the Court decisions that have been handed down up to now, the communities have been awaken. There has been a growing consciousness of the problem. You might liken it to the situation after this Court decided Brown versus Board of Education.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Awaken then persuaded?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Pardon sir?

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Awaken then persuaded?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That's correct, yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: You have a, I don't (Inaudible) is probably the wrong word, but you'd have a powerful argument that you better get doing something state legislatures because you can't any longer convict --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Yes, and the thing that is I think disturbed the amici is that in most jurisdictions where there has been no court decision, there has been no progress. There has been a complete unwillingness to deal with the situation because it is being handled in the jails. It is being handled inadequately and inhumanely, but nevertheless you sweep it under the rock.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What I understand in what you're doing would be some of the broad statement rather than the (Inaudible) constitution argument apparently --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Your Honor I'd --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you saying that the power to (Inaudible) --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Well I would be happy to deal with that right at this point. The basic argument is that the punishment under the criminal law of an inherent universal and necessary symptom of an illness, in this case the illness being alcoholism and the symptom being public intoxication is unconstitutional through, I might say an extension of the holding of this Court in Robinson versus California. We believe that a statute which does not purport to offer treatment that will rehabilitate people that will prevent the type of recidivism that is so clearly shown in this case by appellant Powell, has no logic, has no humanity and is manifestly cruel. Now there is, in a sense, there's no authority that we can cite beyond the case in this Court of Robinson versus California.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: You're relying, in other words, entirely on the Eighth Amendment as it might be incorporated in the Fourteenth, that right?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That is entirely correct, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: No Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The punishment being applied on to the (Inaudible).

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: The punishment is the conviction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- the conviction?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Yes, in our opinion it would make no difference whether this case, Powell, had been given two years in jail, which happened in Driver or have a suspended sentence which happened in Easter. It makes no difference from a constitutional standpoint. What we say is that the criminal law is inappropriate, ineffective and inhumane as a result. This is the very strong feeling not just of the appellant and his counsel, but of the law enforcement and the medical treatment personnel represented on the brief for the amici curiae.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's the stage of the (Inaudible)?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Oh! In other words, you're going back to the original cause?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible) right to say federal stated to (Inaudible).

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: I would say that I would be willing to concede that a state could make a legislative determination and that it might be reviewable in court and it might be sufficient and in my view probably would be sufficient evidence medically to overturn. That issue isn't in this case because the Texas State Legislature has specifically found as a legislative fact that alcoholism is an illness and that an alcoholic drinks involuntarily. He has lost self control over the use of alcohol. That is true in at least 40 states. And as a matter of fact, one of the amici curiae in this case is the North American Association of Alcoholism programs, which represents all of the state tax supported agencies that have been set up by the state legislatures to deal with this problem under state statutes.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: What kind of statutes?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: They are typical --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: They are I would say somewhat similar to the Texas statute. They define an alcoholic. They say that an alcoholic should be given treatment and that alcoholism is an illness.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: There are 40 states you say?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: At least. There're maybe closer to -- I say at least 40 because it may be 45, maybe even close to the 50.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: How many of them are like Texas and that they haven't implemented it or funded it?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That is a very difficult question because each one will have implemented or funded it to a different degree, but certainly I would say that none has done an adequate job. I think I can say that flatly, and I don't think that any one of those programs would disagree with me. They have not been able to generate the interest again, because the problem has been hidden, and if I could say just a few concluding words to save time for rebuttal. The amici in this case who I say have the responsibility under state laws both for law enforcement and for treatment of ill persons, believe very strongly that the future of alcoholism treatment rest in this case. And we certainly urge that this case -- that in this case that the Court keep the door open for the opportunity for treatment that we wish to have.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Could I ask you just one more question? When you say your argument is limited to chronic alcoholic --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: -- could you indicate what you mean by that chronic alcoholic?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: A chronic alcoholic is defined under the Texas state statute which we have cited in our brief Your Honor, as a person who has lost self-control over the use of alcoholism.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: So within -- it includes the idea of addiction that alcohol is --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Yes I would also cite, and I think this is important, in appendix B in our --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Just a question of a person who drinks a lot and often and gets drunk often.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Certainly not. A person -- the -- if I can perhaps define the laws of --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: -- chronic alcoholism which isn't addictive or isn't -- that doesn't represent addiction.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: No, I think I can give you a one sentence definition from a recent report. This I think is the best definition in lay terminology. It is from the 1967 report to the nation of a special study group that had studied the problem for five years under a grant from the National Institute of Mental Health.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Is that cited in you brief?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: It is cited in the brief, on page 39 is a definition though which is not cited in the brief and I therefore would like to read it. Alcoholism is defined as a condition in which the individual has lost control over his alcohol intake in the sense that he is consistently unable to refrain from drinking or to stop drinking before getting intoxicated. He can't --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Or?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Or to stop drinking before getting intoxicated. Now obviously if he has no money --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: There's quite a difference between those two.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Yes there is but it's both. He can't either --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Well let's just assume though that we're talking about a chronic alcoholic who only qualifies under the last part of that definition.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Then quite clearly, he is not the man we're talking about. If, in my view, and I think in the prevailing medical view that man would not be a chronic alcoholic, unless he both is unable to avoid drinking and unable to stop once started then that man is not a chronic alcoholic, he --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Can you incite a state that may have the power to apply its criminal laws to the alcoholic who falls in that ladder category?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Well, he isn't an alcoholic Your Honor, he is -- what we would call a voluntary drinker.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: Has to -- the man has to be both. Those are not alternative definitions.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That's right. And as Dr.Wade testified in the case below and as was found as a matter of fact, although appellant knew he was drinking in the same way, I guess that a person who involuntarily confesses as the last case before this Court shows.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: He knew he was confessing.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: He knew -- he knows he's confessing but he's powerless to avoid it and the alcoholic is powerless to avoid either drinking or he is powerless -- and he is powerless to avoid stopping. I'm sorry, he is powerless to stop drinking.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: There's a different definition, somewhat different definition on page 10 of the petitioner's brief. The definition apparently formulated by the National Institute of Mental Health of the Department of Health, Education and Welfare of 1963.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: There are many --

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well this seems to be -- well is this a different definition? A person who is powerless to stop drinking and who's drinking seriously alters his normal living pattern, period. That is different?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Well, the difficulty is this, that there are many different definitions in a sense for many different purposes. Some people would say that an alcoholic is a person who drinks to an extent that he -- it has harmed his life. That is perhaps the broadest definition and it is functional in some situations where you want to defraud -- I'm sorry, define alcoholism broadly for purposes of getting treatment to his larger population as possible. This would include the incipient alcoholic, but the true chronic addictive alcoholic is the one we're talking about. And the chronic alcoholic as opposed to the incipient alcoholic would have to have, in certainly our case, the appellant's case and the amici agree, is based upon an inability to abstain and an inability to stop. It is a --

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well the Texas statutory definition is different again.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: It says loss of self control.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: Or, or while chronically and habitually under the influence of alcoholic beverages endangers public morals, health, safety or welfare.

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That is an un-equated --

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: And quite a new thought, isn't it?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Yes, and that stands from a 1947 District of Columbia statute, which frankly was enacted before as much as is now know was known.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: But it is --

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: It is un-equated.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- it is the definition of this so called disease in the state from which this case comes, isn't it?

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: That is correct. And in all the court decisions that have come down on the question of alcoholism, Your Honor, the courts have been careful to point out that they were talking about the first part of that definition because that part of that the definition was in the D.C. case. It was in the Fourth Circuit case and it's been in all of the cases.Frankly, it's just been ignored by the courts as un-equated.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: PETER BARTON HUTT
turns.text: Yes that's --

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. We believe that the issue presented by this appeal is a more narrow one and in view of the last statements of counsel for amici, I'm not sure that this case raises the constitutional question they wish to raise at all. We believe that the issue is whether an excessive drinker who has the ability to abstain may be convicted of public intoxication consistently with the Eighth and Fourteenth Amendments. Now if indeed it is conceded that a person who has the power to abstain would not be subject to this constitutional immunity, we feel that this case does not raise the question sought at all. The record is very short. The critical witness on the question of the appellant's ability to abstain was the only psychiatrist called, he was called by the defendant. The psychiatrist, the Dr.David Wade who was amply qualified as an expert in the field of diagnosis and treatment of alcoholics for a period of over 25 years, did testify on direct examination that the appellant has an uncontrollable compulsion to drink and that his going in public is involuntary. However, on cross-examination this general assertion was substantially qualified and clarified, and I'm reading form the record on page 26, referring to the appellant. Question “But at the time that he takes the first drink from a sober condition, would you say that it is a voluntary exercise of his will?” Answer “Yes. I would like to answer that question yes, but I would like to say that we must take into account” --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: What page is that?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I'm reading on page 26 of the --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Oh Yes! Thank you.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: And we must take into account that these individuals have a compulsion and this compulsion while not completely overpowering is a very strong influence and exceedingly strong influence. And this compulsion coupled with the firm belief in their mind that they're going to be able to handle it from now on causes their judgment to be somewhat clouded.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: You're asking us, in other words, to ignore or disregard the fact findings of the trial court, are you?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I am, I am Your Honor.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: Rather not request on the part of the respondent of the case, isn't it?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: It is, obviously, we are not completely pleased with the findings of fact of the trial court.

turns.name: JUTICE STEWART
turns.text: No.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I gather that Texas has a practice which in my own experience is I would say unusual of allowing counsel who does not prevail to submit proposed findings of fact. In this case, defense counsel submitted proposed findings of fact on the afternoon this case was tried and the judge signed them. But our position is that there's no substantial evidence in this record to support the conclusion that the defendant lack power to abstain from drinking entirely. Although we do concede that there's substantial evidence in the record to sustain a conclusion that he could not drink moderately, the ones he started --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Well if we were to go behind that finding of fact on the basis of this man's track record, I guess we'd have to conclude that either he had a -- he wasn't able to control himself or didn't try very hard, is that right?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I would think that --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: He sure has a formidable track record, doesn't he?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: He has a record of 73 arrests over a period of 16 years which is to say of being arrested between four and --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: That's above average isn't it?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I don't know the local conditions, but I would think it's above average.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: There's no doubt that this person frequently drank to excess. There's no doubt about that at all, but the astonishing thing is that some people do want to drink to excess occasionally and sometimes even frequently, that some people do want to adopt a way of life involving excessive use of alcohol. Now it is quite common to characterize that as loss of control because the person doing the characterizing is ordinarily evaluating them from quite a different set of values. The expert says “Well if I were that guy, I would not continue to drink in view of the consequences on his total life, but I don't think that means that the man's behavior is involuntary.” As a matter of fact, studies by interview of these people, indicate that many of them say “I like to drink” and many of them say “I've never tried to stop” but ought that to immunize them. Now the record in this case also includes a statement of Dr.Wade in terms of his taking the history of the appellant that the appellant had never been referred to Alcoholics Anonymous. He didn't go beyond that, but I assume by that, that he was being polite in saying that the defendant had not sought the help of Alcoholics Anonymous. Well why not? I assume that the defendant for whatever reason had a desire to continue his present way of life.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you accept that the findings of fact --

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Pardon me Your Honor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If you accept the findings of fact and recognize it --

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Well, we're prepared to make a constitutional argument even accepting the findings of fact. May I allude to that in just a moment? I would like to say that the appellant did make --

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: -- accept those findings of fact on the constitutional --

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I believe that the court can go behind those findings of fact Your Honor for a variety of reasons.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Your theory seems --

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Well in the first place, they're not findings of specific historical fact. They are conclusions which are interfused with standards which are proposed for constitutional adjudication. The first two findings of fact, the critical ones, are very generalized statements about the nature of chronic alcoholism. So it seems to me they're -- they contain for purposes of this appeal about as much law as fact, and furthermore, it seems to me that this Court is capable of taking judicial notice of facts of this sort. I would think it astonishing --

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Now the basic, since your experts know it --

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Well I believe that the court is the position to become as much experts in alcoholism as the trial court was in Texas.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well how many drunks do you think that judges ran across in Texas?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I have no knowledge of that Your Honor.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, how many times do you have this particular one?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I have no knowledge of that.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, it would be few wouldn't it?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Well I'd like to answer Your Honor, but I have no information on which to make any statement.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, this is the Court that gets the drunks?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I think this is a case or I believe this is a case on appeal Your Honor, and how many drunks it gets, I do not know. It was appealed from the lower court to --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: And he saw --

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: -- to what I assume is a trial court of general jurisdiction.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: And he saw the man, the petitioner here?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I assume he saw the petitioner, right.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Well it would be pretty hard to convict him without seeing him, wouldn't it? He did see him.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: He did see him.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: And he watched him.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: And he had the benefit of that.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: You haven't seen him.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I have not seen him.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: I haven't seen him.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: No.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: None of us have seen him, and you say we should disregard the court's finding.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Well I believe conclusions with respect to volitional capacity are not or cannot be made on the basis of seeing someone.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: But your testimony observed the witnesses too, didn't you?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: That is correct, but on the basis of the testimony we assert these findings are not supported by substantial evidence.

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: Because of the one question on the record, one question on broad --

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Well, it seems to me that the medical witness was being extremely careful on cross-examination and he explained himself --

turns.name: THURGOOD MARSHALL
turns.text: And extremely careless on direct examination.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: The direct examination was primarily conducted by leading questions. I think he was trying -- I assume he was trying to give honest answers to both counsel Your Honor, but I think that I do think that the cross-examination is significant. I think it's also significant that during the redirect examination, there was no attempt the physician's statement on cross-examination. Now the defendant, at one time, was asked “Can you stop drinking” and he said “No.” That's the only other evidence in the record, but I would say that that is an ambiguous question because it doesn't indicate whether that means stop after you start or stop in the sense of completely abstaining. Now it's my understanding that typical members of Alcoholics Anonymous for example characterize loss of control as loss of control after they take their first drink. And Dr.Wade was making this statement not only with respect to the appellant but with respect -- he said the appellant was fairly typical of alcoholics. And we have other authorities who also indicate that the dominant form of American alcoholism contains this phenomenon, namely an inability to drink moderately but an ability to completely abstain. As to the finding -- well I --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Do I understand from your argument that it's critical to your case to upset the findings of the District Court?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: No Your Honor. We contend that even if the findings are sustained that the conviction ought to be affirmed.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: On the basis of the -- that the trial court said?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Pardon me Your Honor?

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: On the basis that the trial court stated?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: No. Well perhaps yes Your Honor, I think I would have to change my answer and say yes on the basis that the trail court stated.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: That alcoholism just isn't a defense to some other act?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir. The defendant's -- the appellant's argument seems to us to be this, that there exists a disease and I get the impression that is looked upon almost a unitary disease called chronic alcoholism. And that this destroyed his will to abstain, his will to drink moderately, his will to avoid appearing in public in a condition of intoxication and therefore he ought to be treated not as a responsible human being but rather as an ill person who is acting without volition. At the outset, it ought to be noted that this argument, if accepted by the Court, would have revolutionary implications for the criminal law. For example, the alcoholic driver who drives to another bar or to a liquor store to get a bottle of liquor would presumably have a defense of chronic alcoholism to a charge of driving while under the influence of intoxicating liquor or even perhaps to negligent homicide.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well I don't suppose -- at least that issue is not here, is it?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I believe it is Your Honor if we --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: But the conviction here is only for public drunkenness and what you're now suggesting, as I understand it, is that it logically must follow if it's unconstitutional to convict for public -- convict to chronic alcoholic to public drunkenness. Then it also must be logically unconstitutional to convict the chronic alcoholic, who while drunk, commits a murder or robbery or steals an automobile or less, is that it?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Not in every situation, but where there's a close cause or relation between his chronic alcoholism and the crime for which he's charged, the answer would have to be yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well I of it's an addictive kind of alcoholism that I understand from your adversary is the issue that they presented. Did I understand you to suggest that necessary that would mean if the addictive alcoholic committed a robbery or a murder or a rape while drunk, you suggesting that he'd have to be --

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Well, it would also has to be shown presumably an adequate cause or relationship between his alcoholism and his robbery or rape.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: You're just --

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: But I'm assuming that. Assuming that, but I agree.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: But you were assuming it.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: No, but you suggested that if in order to get some more whiskey which he has a compulsion to get, he got in his car and drove to the liquor store and on that trip had an accident, perhaps this ruling would reach that situation?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: That's correct Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well not carrying one step further. Are you suggesting perhaps it would also rule a situation if he got to the store, he had no money and he killed the proprietor in order to get a bottle of liquor?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Assuming an adequate relationship between the homicide and his drive to get --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well I've given you the cause of --

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I would think so, yes Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And you think any ruling as suggested here would have that necessary consequence?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I think a ruling on the basis of the sort of volitional criteria that they suggest as a basis for constitutional adjudication would have that consequence.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Volitional? I understood it was not -- it was un-volitional criteria.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: Well, volitional criteria, by that I meant requiring volition in some sense independent of his drive to obtain liquor. I'm not disputing it here Your Honor. It seems to us also that the principle which they urge would also a fortiori be applicable to crimes by a narcotic cabinet. Immediately go on presumably would be the possession of narcotic statute to which under the Uniform Narcotics Act is fairly general throughout the nation. If the person accused were a narcotics addict and presumably sale of narcotics by an addict in order to support his habit would also be exculpated. And theft and prostitution and robbery by an addict in order to support his habit would presumably also, under the principle urged here, be exculpated. Now with respect to the relationship between alcoholism and other crimes, we have not -- we have presented some information in our brief on page 17 and 18 a long study in Columbus, Ohio indicated 64 persons who were arrested for felonies were intoxicated. Now that was not necessarily chronic alcoholics. It would include presumably some chronic alcoholics and undetermined member. In the study in Sing Sing prison indicated that 22% of their prisoners were habitually tampered. Well there is a strong relationship between alcohol and other crimes because as is well known it does relax one's inhibitions. And I don't think it can be said that we're only talking about harmless passive skid row derelicts because the range of chronic alcoholics or people diagnosed chronic alcoholics extends through all types of personalities; sociopaths, passive aggressive personality, schizophrenics, etcetera. We're not dealing here with the homogenous group.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Excuse me, I gather the part of findings of the lower court is that appearing in public while drunk is a recurring or unavoidable symptom of alcoholism.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I assume that yes Your Honor. That is --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Spoke a lot of alcoholics that don't go out on the public street.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I assume that must be --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Must be in bars, do they stay at home or somewhere else? But I think -- do you understand the key to this -- the basis for this statement is that what they really mean is the person has lost his control, not that his alcohol simply drives him to go out on the street rather than to stay at home, because he just losses his control and he's appearing everywhere.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I assume that's their position, yes.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: If that said, this is a sort of argument you're making then as to the extent of this holding that if it's just the lack of control that alcoholism causes, then the same argument would apply to other crimes?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I would think so Your Honor.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Mr. Robinson, are you familiar with the police practices in -- where is this, Travis County?

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I am not Your Honor.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: I see, I wondered whether its customary for the policeman who arrested the drunk to appear in court the next morning if that's when the fellow is brought up about to be tried and sentenced.

turns.name: DAVID ROBINSON, JR.
turns.text: I have no information Your Honor with respect to that. I am not a resident of Texas myself.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
